title: A polynomial expression for the Hilbert series of the quotient ring of diagonal coinvariants
overall_summary: The paper starts from Haiman's Macdonald-polynomial formula for the Frobenius series of the two-variable diagonal coinvariant ring and transforms the resulting rational expression for its bigraded Hilbert series into an explicit polynomial formula. The main technical device is a Garsia-Zabrocki summation identity for Macdonald Pieri coefficients, combined with repeated application of e_1^‚ä• to remove corners of partitions. The resulting polynomial is expressed as a weighted sum over upper-triangular Tesler matrices and equivalently as a constant term or coefficient extraction from a universal Laurent product. The paper also extends the same formulas to Haiman's ‚àá^m e_n modules by changing the Tesler hook-sum vector from (1,1,...,1) to (1,m,m,...,m). For the original problem, the most relevant content is the Tesler/constant-term technology for Hilbert-series extraction and the paper's Section 5.3 discussion of Schur-positive refinements and Bergeron's conjectural Schur/h-basis description for diagonal coinvariants in arbitrarily many grading variables.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Haiman character formula and Macdonald rational Hilbert-series expansion, cited in Sections 1, 2, and 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let DR_n = C[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n] modulo the ideal generated by all diagonal power sums ‚àë_{i=1}^n x_i^h y_i^k with h,k ‚â• 0 and h+k > 0, with S_n acting diagonally by simultaneous permutation of the x and y variables. Let HÃÉ_¬µ(X;q,t) be the modified Macdonald polynomial indexed by a partition ¬µ of n, and define ‚àá by ‚àá HÃÉ_¬µ = T_¬µ HÃÉ_¬µ, where T_¬µ = t^{n(¬µ)} q^{n(¬µ‚Ä≤)} and n(¬µ) = ‚àë_i (i‚àí1)¬µ_i. Haiman's theorem, as used in the paper, says that the Frobenius series of DR_n is ‚àá e_n, hence its bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(DR_n) = ‚ü®‚àá e_n, h_1^n‚ü© with respect to the Hall scalar product. For a Ferrers square s of ¬µ, let l(s), a(s), l‚Ä≤(s), a‚Ä≤(s) be its leg, arm, coleg, and coarm. Put M = (1‚àíq)(1‚àít), B_¬µ = ‚àë_{s in ¬µ} t^{l‚Ä≤(s)} q^{a‚Ä≤(s)}, ŒÝ_¬µ = ‚àè_{s in ¬µ, s ‚âÝ (0,0)} (1‚àít^{l‚Ä≤(s)} q^{a‚Ä≤(s)}), w_¬µ = ‚àè_{s in ¬µ} (q^{a(s)}‚àít^{l(s)+1})(t^{l(s)}‚àíq^{a(s)+1}), and F_¬µ = ‚ü®HÃÉ_¬µ,h_1^n‚ü©. The known expansion e_n = ‚àë_{¬µ‚ä¢n} HÃÉ_¬µ M ŒÝ_¬µ B_¬µ / w_¬µ implies Hilb(DR_n) = ‚àë_{¬µ‚ä¢n} T_¬µ F_¬µ M ŒÝ_¬µ B_¬µ / w_¬µ. More generally, for every positive integer r, Haiman's result gives an S_n-module DR_n^(r) whose Frobenius series is ‚àá^r e_n, and therefore Hilb(DR_n^(r)) = ‚ü®‚àá^r e_n,h_1^n‚ü© = ‚àë_{¬µ‚ä¢n} T_¬µ^r F_¬µ M ŒÝ_¬µ B_¬µ / w_¬µ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Haiman Frobenius statement is quoted rather than proved. The derivation of the displayed rational Hilbert-series formula is immediate from the modified Macdonald expansion of e_n: apply ‚àá, or ‚àá^r in the higher case, which multiplies HÃÉ_¬µ by T_¬µ or T_¬µ^r, then take the Hall scalar product with h_1^n. The scalar product contributes F_¬µ = ‚ü®HÃÉ_¬µ,h_1^n‚ü© and leaves the coefficients M ŒÝ_¬µ B_¬µ / w_¬µ unchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Macdonald Pieri adjunction and Garsia-Zabrocki summation formula, Section 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a symmetric function and let f^‚ä• denote the operator adjoint to multiplication by f under the Hall scalar product. If ŒΩ is obtained from ¬µ by removing one corner square, write ŒΩ ‚Üí ¬µ, equivalently ¬µ ‚Üê ŒΩ. Define generalized skew Pieri coefficients c^{f^‚ä•}_{¬µ,ŒΩ}(q,t) and Pieri coefficients d^f_{¬µ,ŒΩ}(q,t) by f^‚ä• HÃÉ_¬µ = ‚àë_{ŒΩ:ŒΩ‚Üí¬µ} c^{f^‚ä•}_{¬µ,ŒΩ} HÃÉ_ŒΩ and f HÃÉ_ŒΩ = ‚àë_{¬µ:¬µ‚ÜêŒΩ} d^f_{¬µ,ŒΩ} HÃÉ_¬µ. The paper uses the relation c^{f^‚ä•}_{¬µ,ŒΩ} w_ŒΩ = d^{œâ f[X/M]}_{¬µ,ŒΩ} w_¬µ, where œâ is the standard involution on symmetric functions and M=(1‚àíq)(1‚àít). For f=e_1=h_1, abbreviate c_{¬µ,ŒΩ}=c^{h_1^‚ä•}_{¬µ,ŒΩ} and d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}=d^{h_1[X/M]}_{¬µ,ŒΩ}. For a fixed partition ŒΩ and for each addable-corner extension ¬µ‚ÜêŒΩ, let T = T_¬µ/T_ŒΩ. Then the Garsia-Zabrocki summation identity used in the paper is ‚àë_{¬µ:¬µ‚ÜêŒΩ} d_{¬µ,ŒΩ} T^k = 1/M for k=0, and ‚àë_{¬µ:¬µ‚ÜêŒΩ} d_{¬µ,ŒΩ} T^k = (‚àí1)^{k‚àí1} e_{k‚àí1}[M B_ŒΩ ‚àí 1]/M for k ‚â• 1, where e_j[ ] denotes plethystic substitution.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The adjunction relation is cited from Garsia-Haglund, and the summation identity is cited from Garsia-Zabrocki, Bergeron-Garsia-Haiman-Tesler, and Garsia. The paper's subsequent arguments use these identities as black boxes. The key role of the adjunction relation is that, after replacing F_¬µ by a sum over F_ŒΩ via e_1^‚ä•, it converts c_{¬µ,ŒΩ}/w_¬µ into d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}/w_ŒΩ, allowing all dependence on the newly added corner to be summed by the Garsia-Zabrocki formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1, summation with the factor 1‚àíT
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a partition ŒΩ. With M=(1‚àíq)(1‚àít), B_ŒΩ = ‚àë_{s in ŒΩ} t^{l‚Ä≤(s)}q^{a‚Ä≤(s)}, d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}=d^{h_1[X/M]}_{¬µ,ŒΩ}, and T=T_¬µ/T_ŒΩ for each ¬µ obtained by adding one corner to ŒΩ, one has ‚àë_{¬µ:¬µ‚ÜêŒΩ} d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}(1‚àíT)T^k = 0 if k=0, and ‚àë_{¬µ:¬µ‚ÜêŒΩ} d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}(1‚àíT)T^k = (‚àí1)^{k‚àí1} e_k[M B_ŒΩ]/M if k ‚â• 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For k=0, subtract the k=1 case of the Garsia-Zabrocki identity from the k=0 case: both sums equal 1/M. For k‚â•1, use the plethystic addition formula e_r[X‚àíY] = ‚àë_{j=0}^r e_j[X] e_{r‚àíj}[‚àíY] and the identity f[‚àíX] = (‚àí1)^{deg f} œâ f[X] for homogeneous f. This gives e_{k‚àí1}[M B_ŒΩ‚àí1] = ‚àë_{j=0}^{k‚àí1} e_j[M B_ŒΩ](‚àí1)^{k‚àí1‚àíj}. Then compute M‚àë d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}(1‚àíT)T^k as M‚àë d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}T^k ‚àí M‚àë d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}T^{k+1}. Substituting the Garsia-Zabrocki formulas for the two power sums causes all terms in the two finite alternating sums to cancel except the final e_k[M B_ŒΩ] term, yielding (‚àí1)^{k‚àí1} e_k[M B_ŒΩ].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2, evaluation of e_k[M] as a q,t-integer
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer k ‚â• 1 and M=(1‚àíq)(1‚àít), the plethystic elementary symmetric function satisfies (‚àí1)^{k‚àí1} e_k[M]/M = (t^k‚àíq^k)/(t‚àíq). The right side is the q,t-analogue [k]_{q,t} = t^{k‚àí1}+t^{k‚àí2}q+...+q^{k‚àí1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the Cauchy identity e_k[XY] = ‚àë_{Œª‚ä¢k} s_Œª[X] s_{Œª‚Ä≤}[Y] with X=1‚àíq and Y=1‚àít. The evaluation s_Œª[1‚àíz] vanishes unless Œª is a hook Œª=(k‚àír,1^r), in which case it equals (‚àíz)^r(1‚àíz). Therefore e_k[M] = ‚àë_{r=0}^{k‚àí1} (‚àíq)^r(1‚àíq)(‚àít)^{k‚àí1‚àír}(1‚àít). Dividing by M=(1‚àíq)(1‚àít) and multiplying by (‚àí1)^{k‚àí1} gives ‚àë_{r=0}^{k‚àí1} q^r t^{k‚àí1‚àír} = (t^k‚àíq^k)/(t‚àíq).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, recursive polynomial formula for Hilb(DR_n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition œÅ, use T_œÅ, B_œÅ, ŒÝ_œÅ, w_œÅ, and F_œÅ as in the Haiman-Macdonald expansion above. Define a_k = (‚àí1)^{k‚àí1} e_k[M]/M and, for each partition œÅ, b_k(œÅ) = (‚àí1)^{k‚àí1} e_k[M B_œÅ]/M. Then for every integer p with 1 ‚â§ p ‚â§ n there is a polynomial A_p(œÅ) in the symbols a_i and b_i(œÅ) such that Hilb(DR_n) = ‚àë_{œÅ‚ä¢n‚àíp+1} T_œÅ F_œÅ M ŒÝ_œÅ A_p(œÅ) / w_œÅ. The initial polynomial is A_1=b_1. To compute A_p from A_{p‚àí1}, replace every occurrence of b_k in A_{p‚àí1} by b_k + T^k a_k ‚àí ‚àë_{j=1}^{k‚àí1} M b_j T^{k‚àíj} a_{k‚àíj}, expand the result as a polynomial ‚àë_j c_j T^j in the auxiliary variable T, and then replace each T^j by b_{j+1}. For example A_1=b_1, A_2=b_1^2+b_2 a_1, and the paper lists A_3=b_1^3+2b_1a_1b_2+a_1^2b_3+a_1b_2b_1‚àíM b_1a_1^2b_2+a_1a_2b_3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from Hilb(DR_n)=‚àë_{¬µ‚ä¢n} T_¬µ F_¬µ MŒÝ_¬µB_¬µ/w_¬µ. Since e_1^‚ä• HÃÉ_¬µ = ‚àë_{ŒΩ‚Üí¬µ} c_{¬µ,ŒΩ} HÃÉ_ŒΩ, pairing with h_1^{n‚àí1} gives F_¬µ=‚àë_{ŒΩ‚Üí¬µ} c_{¬µ,ŒΩ} F_ŒΩ. Substitute this into the Hilbert-series sum and reverse the order of summation, obtaining a sum over ŒΩ‚ä¢n‚àí1 and addable corners ¬µ‚ÜêŒΩ. For such ¬µ, B_¬µ=B_ŒΩ+T and ŒÝ_¬µ=ŒÝ_ŒΩ(1‚àíT), where T=T_¬µ/T_ŒΩ. The Pieri adjunction c_{¬µ,ŒΩ}w_ŒΩ=d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}w_¬µ converts the inner sum to ‚àë_{¬µ‚ÜêŒΩ} d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}(B_ŒΩ+T)(1‚àíT)T, and Lemma 1 evaluates this as b_1(ŒΩ)^2+b_2(ŒΩ)a_1, giving the p=2 case. Iterating the same argument requires rewriting b_k(¬µ) in terms of B_¬µ=B_ŒΩ+T. The plethystic addition formula gives (‚àí1)^{k‚àí1}e_k[M(B_ŒΩ+T)]/M = b_k(ŒΩ)+T^k a_k‚àí‚àë_{j=1}^{k‚àí1} M b_j(ŒΩ)T^{k‚àíj}a_{k‚àíj}. After this substitution into A_{p‚àí1}, the remaining addable-corner sum has the common factor d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}(1‚àíT)T; Lemma 1 then converts each monomial T^j into b_{j+1}. This proves the recursion and the displayed formula by induction on p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, Tesler-matrix expression for the recursive polynomials A_p
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For N ‚â• 1, let Q_N be the set of N by N upper-triangular matrices C=(c_{ij}) with nonnegative integer entries satisfying the hook-sum equations ‚àë_{i=j}^N c_{j i} ‚àí ‚àë_{i=1}^{j‚àí1} c_{i j} = 1 for every j=1,...,N. Let Pos(C) be the multiset of positive entries of C and pos(C)=|Pos(C)|. With a_k=(‚àí1)^{k‚àí1}e_k[M]/M and b_k=b_k(œÅ)=(‚àí1)^{k‚àí1}e_k[M B_œÅ]/M for a fixed partition œÅ, the polynomial A_N(œÅ) from Theorem 1 is A_N(œÅ)=‚àë_{C in Q_N} (‚àíM)^{pos(C)‚àíN} ‚àè_{i: c_{ii}>0} b_{c_{ii}} ‚àè_{i<j: c_{ij}>0} a_{c_{ij}}. Equivalently, for A_p one uses matrices of size p and the exponent pos(C)‚àíp; the paper's proof denotes the matrix size by n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the matrix size. For C in Q_N, every row must contain at least one positive entry, so pos(C)‚àíN counts the number of adjacent pairs of positive entries in rows, with no positive entry between them. Map C to a matrix C‚Ä≤ in Q_{N‚àí1} by adding each entry in the last column to the corresponding diagonal entry and then deleting the last row and last column: c‚Ä≤_{ij}=c_{ij} for j‚âÝi and c‚Ä≤_{ii}=c_{ii}+c_{iN}. Conversely, given W in Q_{N‚àí1}, choose for each i<N an integer k_i with 0‚â§k_i‚â§w_{ii}, set c_{ii}=w_{ii}‚àík_i and c_{iN}=k_i, keep c_{ij}=w_{ij} for i<j<N, and set c_{NN}=1+‚àë_{j=1}^{N‚àí1} c_{jN}. If 0<k_i<w_{ii}, row i gains one additional positive entry, producing one extra factor ‚àíM. These choices exactly mirror the recursive replacement b_k ‚Ü¶ b_k+T^k a_k‚àí‚àë_{j=1}^{k‚àí1}M b_jT^{k‚àíj}a_{k‚àíj}: keeping k_i=0 corresponds to a diagonal b_k, taking k_i=w_{ii} corresponds to an off-diagonal a_k, and splitting into two positive parts corresponds to the ‚àíM b_j a_{k‚àíj} terms. The final diagonal entry c_{NN} records the power of T that Lemma 1 converts into the final b factor.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1, explicit Tesler-matrix polynomial for Hilb(DR_n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Q_n be the set of upper-triangular nonnegative integer n by n matrices satisfying ‚àë_{i=j}^n c_{j i} ‚àí ‚àë_{i=1}^{j‚àí1} c_{i j} = 1 for all j=1,...,n. Then Hilb(DR_n) = ‚àë_{C in Q_n} (‚àíM)^{pos(C)‚àín} ‚àè_{1‚â§i‚â§j‚â§n, c_{ij}>0} [c_{ij}]_{q,t}, where M=(1‚àíq)(1‚àít), pos(C) is the number of positive entries of C, and [k]_{q,t}=(t^k‚àíq^k)/(t‚àíq).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take p=n in Theorem 1, so the outer sum over partitions contains only the single partition (1). For ¬µ=(1), one has B_(1)=1, ŒÝ_(1)=1, w_(1)=M, and F_(1)=1. Lemma 2 gives a_k=[k]_{q,t}, and since B_(1)=1 it also gives b_k=[k]_{q,t}. Substituting these evaluations into the Tesler expression for A_n from Theorem 2 yields exactly the displayed sum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2 and Remark 2, constant-term formula for Hilb(DR_n) and for A_p
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n ‚â• 1, Hilb(DR_n) is the coefficient of z_1 z_2 ... z_n in the Laurent series 1/(‚àíM)^n ‚àè_{i=1}^n ((1‚àíz_i)(1‚àíqt z_i))/((1‚àíq z_i)(1‚àít z_i)) ‚àè_{1‚â§i<j‚â§n} ((1‚àíz_i/z_j)(1‚àíqt z_i/z_j))/((1‚àíq z_i/z_j)(1‚àít z_i/z_j)), where M=(1‚àíq)(1‚àít). Equivalently, with Œ©(E)=‚àë_{r‚â•0} h_r[E] in plethystic notation and Z_p denoting the set of variables {z_1,...,z_p}, the intermediate polynomial A_p(¬µ) satisfies A_p(¬µ) = coefficient of z_1...z_p in 1/(‚àíM)^p Œ©[‚àíM B_¬µ Z_p] ‚àè_{1‚â§i<j‚â§p} ((1‚àíz_i/z_j)(1‚àíqt z_i/z_j))/((1‚àíq z_i/z_j)(1‚àít z_i/z_j)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set f(x)=((1‚àíx)(1‚àíqt x))/((1‚àíq x)(1‚àít x)). Its Taylor expansion at x=0 is f(x)=1‚àíM‚àë_{k‚â•1} x^k [k]_{q,t}. In the product, choosing the term ‚àíM z_i^k[k]_{q,t} from f(z_i) records a diagonal matrix entry c_{ii}=k, while choosing ‚àíM(z_i/z_j)^k[k]_{q,t} from f(z_i/z_j) records an off-diagonal entry c_{ij}=k. The exponent of z_j in the resulting monomial is ‚àë_{i=j}^n c_{j i}‚àí‚àë_{i=1}^{j‚àí1}c_{i j}, precisely the Tesler hook sum. Thus extracting the coefficient of z_1...z_n imposes the Q_n hook-sum equations, and the prefactor 1/(‚àíM)^n converts the product of selected ‚àíM factors into (‚àíM)^{pos(C)‚àín}. The formula for A_p(¬µ) is the same encoding before specializing B_¬µ=1; Œ©[‚àíM B_¬µ Z_p] supplies the diagonal choices with weights b_k(¬µ) instead of [k]_{q,t}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3, operator version using repeated e_1^‚ä•
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer p with 0‚â§p‚â§n‚àí1, the same recursively defined polynomials A_r give an identity in symmetric functions: (e_1^‚ä•)^p ‚àá e_n = ‚àë_{ŒΩ‚ä¢n‚àíp} T_ŒΩ HÃÉ_ŒΩ M ŒÝ_ŒΩ A_{p+1}(ŒΩ) / w_ŒΩ. In particular, e_1^‚ä• ‚àá e_n = ‚àë_{ŒΩ‚ä¢n‚àí1} T_ŒΩ HÃÉ_ŒΩ MŒÝ_ŒΩ/w_ŒΩ ¬∑ (e_1[M B_ŒΩ]/M ¬∑ e_1[M B_ŒΩ]/M ‚àí e_2[M B_ŒΩ]/M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same as the proof of Theorem 1, but one does not take the final scalar product with h_1^{n‚àíp}. Applying e_1^‚ä• to the Macdonald expansion lowers the partition size by one through the coefficients c_{¬µ,ŒΩ}; the adjunction relation changes c_{¬µ,ŒΩ}/w_¬µ to d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}/w_ŒΩ; B_¬µ=B_ŒΩ+T and ŒÝ_¬µ=ŒÝ_ŒΩ(1‚àíT); and Lemma 1 performs the sum over addable corners. Iterating p times gives the stated formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 and Corollary 3, the ‚àá^r or m-parameter extension
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let r be a positive integer. For the S_n-module DR_n^(r) with Frobenius series ‚àá^r e_n, the Hilbert series is Hilb(DR_n^(r))=‚àë_{¬µ‚ä¢n} T_¬µ^r F_¬µ MŒÝ_¬µB_¬µ/w_¬µ. Define A_1^(r)=b_1, where b_k(œÅ)=(‚àí1)^{k‚àí1}e_k[M B_œÅ]/M, and for p>1 compute A_p^(r) from A_{p‚àí1}^(r) by the same substitution b_k ‚Ü¶ b_k+T^k a_k‚àí‚àë_{j=1}^{k‚àí1}M b_jT^{k‚àíj}a_{k‚àíj}, with a_k=(‚àí1)^{k‚àí1}e_k[M]/M, followed by expansion ‚àë c_jT^j and replacement of T^j by b_{j+r}. Then Hilb(DR_n^(r)) = ‚àë_{œÅ‚ä¢n‚àíp+1} T_œÅ^r F_œÅ MŒÝ_œÅ A_p^(r)(œÅ)/w_œÅ for 1‚â§p‚â§n. Let Q_N^(r) be the set of upper-triangular nonnegative integer matrices C=(c_{ij}) satisfying ‚àë_{i=j}^N c_{j i}‚àí‚àë_{i=1}^{j‚àí1}c_{i j}=1 for j=1 and =r for 2‚â§j‚â§N. Then A_p^(r)(œÅ)=‚àë_{C in Q_p^(r)}(‚àíM)^{pos(C)‚àíp} ‚àè_{i:c_{ii}>0} b_{c_{ii}}(œÅ) ‚àè_{i<j:c_{ij}>0} a_{c_{ij}}, and the specialization p=n gives Hilb(DR_n^(r))=‚àë_{C in Q_n^(r)}(‚àíM)^{pos(C)‚àín} ‚àè_{1‚â§i‚â§j‚â§n, c_{ij}>0}[c_{ij}]_{q,t}. Equivalently, Hilb(DR_n^(r)) is the coefficient of z_1 z_2^r z_3^r ... z_n^r in the same Laurent product used in Corollary 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof repeats the derivation of Theorem 1 with one change: because ‚àá^r contributes T_¬µ^r, after writing T_¬µ=T_ŒΩ T, the addable-corner inner sum contains (1‚àíT)T^r instead of (1‚àíT)T. Therefore, after the substitution expressing b_k(¬µ) in terms of b_j(ŒΩ), a_j, and powers of T, a monomial T^j is evaluated by Lemma 1 as b_{j+r}, not b_{j+1}. This proves the modified recursion. The Tesler-matrix proof is identical to Theorem 2 except that the final diagonal entry created at each extension records hook sum r in rows 2 through N, yielding hook-sum vector (1,r,r,...,r). The constant-term form follows exactly as in Corollary 2: the exponent of z_j is the j-th Tesler hook sum, so extracting z_1 z_2^r...z_n^r imposes the defining equations of Q_n^(r).

proof_techniques.name: Recursive corner removal via e_1^‚ä•
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly replaces the difficult scalar F_¬µ=‚ü®HÃÉ_¬µ,h_1^n‚ü© by a sum over one-smaller partitions using e_1^‚ä• HÃÉ_¬µ=‚àë_{ŒΩ‚Üí¬µ} c_{¬µ,ŒΩ}HÃÉ_ŒΩ. Pairing with h_1^{n‚àí1} gives F_¬µ=‚àë_{ŒΩ‚Üí¬µ}c_{¬µ,ŒΩ}F_ŒΩ. This turns the original sum over partitions of n into a sum over partitions of n‚àí1 and addable corners, and iteration lowers the partition size until only the partition (1) remains.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients in multigraded Hilbert series are often accessible through adjoint symmetric-function operators or through repeated removal operations. This paper gives a model example where repeated e_1^‚ä• converts a Hilbert-series scalar product into finite combinatorial data.

proof_techniques.name: Macdonald Pieri summation eliminates rational addable-corner sums
proof_techniques.description: After corner removal, the inner sum over all ways to add a corner to ŒΩ contains Macdonald Pieri coefficients. The adjunction identity c_{¬µ,ŒΩ}w_ŒΩ=d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}w_¬µ converts skew Pieri coefficients into ordinary Pieri coefficients, and the Garsia-Zabrocki summation identity evaluates ‚àë d_{¬µ,ŒΩ}T^k explicitly. Lemma 1 packages the version actually needed in the recursion: sums with an extra factor (1‚àíT) become elementary symmetric functions e_k[M B_ŒΩ]/M.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the target hook coefficients can be expressed through scalar products involving ‚àá or modified Macdonald polynomials, analogous Pieri summations may collapse otherwise complicated sums over partitions. The paper also indicates that extensions beyond h_1-type removals would require corresponding summation formulas for higher Pieri operators.

proof_techniques.name: Plethystic addition to track a newly added corner
proof_techniques.description: A central computational step is the identity B_¬µ=B_ŒΩ+T when ¬µ is obtained from ŒΩ by adding one square, together with the plethystic expansion of b_k(¬µ)=(‚àí1)^{k‚àí1}e_k[M(B_ŒΩ+T)]/M. This gives b_k(¬µ)=b_k(ŒΩ)+T^k a_k‚àí‚àë_{j=1}^{k‚àí1}M b_j(ŒΩ)T^{k‚àíj}a_{k‚àíj}. Thus each recursive step has an explicit algebraic replacement rule on the variables b_k.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For multigraded Schur coefficient extraction, one can look for similar replacement rules when adding a grading variable, adding a corner, or applying an adjoint operator. The hook case may be especially compatible with one-row/one-column plethystic decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Tesler matrices as histories of recursive substitutions
proof_techniques.description: The nonrecursive formula encodes all possible histories of the A_p recursion by upper-triangular matrices. Diagonal positive entries correspond to b-factors, off-diagonal positive entries correspond to a-factors, and every split of a positive diagonal contribution into two positive pieces contributes a factor ‚àíM. The hook-sum equations of the Tesler matrix record the powers of T that are eventually converted into b-factors by Lemma 1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests trying to interpret universal Schur coefficients by refining Tesler matrices, for example by decorating entries or imposing additional conditions corresponding to hook-shaped Schur functions. The terminology hook sum in the paper is unrelated to hook-shaped partitions but may still provide a useful matrix constraint framework.

proof_techniques.name: Constant-term encoding of matrix hook sums
proof_techniques.description: The Tesler sum is converted to a coefficient extraction using the single rational function f(x)=((1‚àíx)(1‚àíqtx))/((1‚àíqx)(1‚àítx))=1‚àíM‚àë_{k‚â•1}x^k[k]_{q,t}. Choosing a term from f(z_i/z_j) creates an off-diagonal entry c_{ij}; choosing a term from f(z_i) creates a diagonal entry c_{ii}. The exponent of z_j in the resulting monomial is exactly the j-th matrix hook sum, so coefficient extraction enforces the desired hook-sum vector.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Schur coefficients can often be extracted by pairing with symmetric functions or by constant-term identities. The kernel in this paper may be a starting point for deriving coefficient formulas in the grading variables, especially after symmetrizing or applying Cauchy identities.

proof_techniques.name: Schur reinterpretation of the Tesler weights
proof_techniques.description: The paper observes that the positive factor [k]_{q,t} in the Tesler formula is the Schur function s_{k‚àí1} evaluated in the two variables {q,t}, while ‚àíM=t+q‚àí1‚àíqt equals s_1‚àí1‚àís_{1,1} in those same variables. Therefore the entire Tesler expression can be expanded in the Schur basis in the grading variables, with cancellations of Schur functions having more than two parts when only {q,t} are present.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is explicitly about Schur coefficients c_Œª(n) in arbitrarily many grading variables. This observation is one of the paper's direct bridges from the q,t Hilbert series to Schur-basis questions and may be useful for isolating hook-shaped Schur coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: The ‚àá^r parameter as a hook-sum shift
proof_techniques.description: In the paper's higher ‚àá^r setting, no new Laurent kernel is needed. The only changes are that the recursive replacement T^j ‚Ü¶ b_{j+1} becomes T^j ‚Ü¶ b_{j+r}, and the Tesler hook-sum vector changes from (1,1,...,1) to (1,r,r,...,r). The constant-term formula changes only in the target monomial, from z_1...z_n to z_1 z_2^r...z_n^r.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the paper's r is the power in ‚àá^r rather than automatically the number of sets of variables in the original problem, this shows how a parameter in a diagonal-coinvariant Hilbert series can sometimes be represented purely by changing a coefficient-extraction vector. A similar mechanism might exist for the number of grading variables or for hook Schur coefficient extraction.

other_useful_info: The paper records the Haglund-Loehr parking-function conjecture Hilb(DR_n)=‚àë_œÄ q^{dinv(œÄ)}t^{area(œÄ)}, where œÄ ranges over parking functions, and notes possible correspondences between restricted Tesler sums and parking functions: matrices with c_11=1 reproduce Hilb(DR_{n‚àí1}), and Maple computations suggest that matrices with c_{1i}=1 correspond to parking functions whose bottom-row car is n for i=1 and i‚àí1 for i>1. Section 5.1 conjectures a broader positivity statement for the same constant-term kernel: if Œ±=(Œ±_1,...,Œ±_n) is positive and nondecreasing, then the coefficient of z_1^{Œ±_1}...z_n^{Œ±_n} is in N[q,t], equivalently weighted Tesler matrices with hook sums Œ± have a positive q,t sum; the paper proves the q=0 specialization after reversing Œ± to a partition ¬µ, obtaining Q_¬µ[‚àí1;t]/(t‚àí1)^n = [n]!_t t^{n(¬µ)+|¬µ|‚àíbinom(n+1,2)} via a Hall-Littlewood evaluation. Remark 4 says that when Œ±_1=1, the coefficient with hook sums Œ± can be generated operatorially by starting with ‚àá^{Œ±_2}e_n, applying e_1^‚ä•, then applying ‚àá^{Œ±_3‚àíŒ±_2}, applying e_1^‚ä• again, and continuing. Section 5.3 is especially relevant to Schur expansions: the paper observes [k]_{q,t}=s_{k‚àí1}({q,t}) and ‚àíM=s_1‚àí1‚àís_{1,1}; after substituting these into the Tesler formula and cancelling Schur functions with more than two parts, computations suggest Schur-positivity. The same section cites F. Bergeron's stronger conjecture that Hilb(DR_n)=‚àë_{œÉ‚ààS_n} h_{Œª(œÉ)}({q,t}) for suitable partitions Œª(œÉ), and that evaluating this same h-basis sum in variables {q_1,...,q_k} gives the Hilbert series of diagonal coinvariants in k sets of variables for every k‚â•1. The paper notes that extending its integrality method from ‚ü®‚àáe_n,h_1^n‚ü© to general ‚ü®‚àáe_n,h_Œª‚ü© would require analogues of the Pieri summation formula for coefficients d^{e_k[X/M]}_{¬µ,ŒΩ}.
why_relevant: Transforms Haiman's rational-function Hilbert-series formula for diagonal coinvariants into an explicit polynomial and gives constant-term formulations. The paper provides useful algebraic and symmetric-function techniques for extracting Hilbert-series coefficients.
